content.problemStatement:
Zanka finds fun in everyday simple things. One fine day he got interested in a very trivial sequence. Given a natural number kkk, he considers the sequence Ai=k+i2A_i=k+i^2Ai‚Äã=k+i2 so that the terms are

k+1,k+4,k+9,k+16,‚Ä¶k+1,k+4,k+9,k+16, \ldots k+1,k+4,k+9,k+16,‚Ä¶

Now to make things more interesting, he considers the gcd‚Å°\gcdgcd of consecutive terms in the sequence, then takes the sum of the first 2k2k2k values. More formally, he wants to compute

‚àëi=12kgcd‚Å°(Ai,Ai+1)\sum_{i=1}^{2k} \gcd(A_i,A_{i+1})‚àëi=12k‚Äãgcd(Ai‚Äã,Ai+1‚Äã)

Denote this sum by SSS. Zanka wants you to print  the number SSS for each kkk.

###Input


The first line contains an integer TTT, the number of test cases. Descriptions of test cases follow.
The only line of each test case contains a single integer kkk.
###Output
For each test case, output a single line containing the sum SSS for the given kkk.

###Constraints


1‚â§T‚â§1061 \leq T \leq 10^61‚â§T‚â§106
1‚â§k‚â§1061 \leq k \leq 10^61‚â§k‚â§106
###Subtasks
**Subtask #1 (20 points):** t‚â§103t \leq 10^3t‚â§103,k‚â§103k \leq 10^3k‚â§103

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The first 2k+12k+12k+1 terms of the sequence AAA are 2,5,102, 5, 102,5,10.
So the answer is gcd‚Å°(2,5)+gcd‚Å°(5,10)=1+5=6\gcd(2,5) + \gcd(5,10) = 1+5=6gcd(2,5)+gcd(5,10)=1+5=6
